                 AND EARNINGS
                                                       VOL.     SO         NO. 7 JANUARY 1974

                  Joseph M. Finerty, Editor
                  Gloria P. Green, Associate Editor
                  James A. McCall, Associate Editor



                                                               Subscription price increase
                             Beginning January 1974, the cost of subscribing to Employment and Earnings has been raised to
                         $28.10 a year. The additional cost for mailing to foreign countries is $7.05 a year. Single copies cost
                         $2.35 each.
                             The Government Printing Office, which handles printing and distribution of Employment and Earnings
                         and other U.S. Government publications, has placed the higher prices into effect to cover increasing costs of
                         its operations, including higher postage rates.
                             The Bureau of Labor Statistics of the Department of Labor, which publishes Employment and Earnings
                         and pays research, editorial, and production costs out of its budget, has no voice in setting subscription prices
                         and does not receive any of the sales revenue.

                                                         The Year in Review



               The Nation's employment situation during 1973 was             annual average basis, and by the last quarter, was 4.7 per-
           characterized by strong employment growth and a de-               cent, marking the ninth successive quarter in which the
           cline in unemployment. On an annual average basis, the            rate had dropped (despite the increase in unemployment
           number of employed persons increased by 2.7 million,              in the last 2 months of the year).
           and the overall jobless rate moved down steadily from a               Each of the major age-sex groups in the labor Torce

           the fourth quarter of 1973. However, after reaching a             less rate for adult men averaged 3.2 percent in 1973,

           ment rate rose to 4.7 percent in November and to                  down to 3.0 percent by the fourth quarter, its lowest

             tAs noted on page 7 of the January 1972 issue of this magazine, the series does not include earned pay withheld as a consequence of delays caused by the wage freeze in effect between August 15
          and November 14, 1971, and by administrative procedures of the Pay Board. These delays resulted from compliance with the Government's Economic Stabilization Program. Because these withheld
          payments were substantial and fell due under a significant number of new labor-management contracts in that industry,they affected a large proportion of SIC 372. The series including these pay-
          ments for September 1971 - March 1973 are shown below.
             A 17 -cent delayed payment for employees in a number of large companies was pending as a consequence of litigation in the courts arising out of the Government's Economic Stabilization Pro
          gram. The issue is undergoing further litigation. These data will later be revised, if necessary, to include this additional delayed payment.

             Under cooperative arrangements with State agencies, the
                                                                               CONCEPTS
         respondent fills out a single employment or job vacancy-labor
         turnover reporting form, which is then used for national, State,
         and area estimates. This eliminates duplicate reporting on the        Industrial classification
         part of respondents and, together with the use of identical
         techniques at the national and State levels, insures maximum              Establishments reporting on Form BLS 790 and Form DL
         comparability of estimates.                                           1219 are classified into industries on the basis of their principal
              State agencies mail the forms to the establishments and          product or activity determined from information on annual sales
          examine the returns for consistency, accuracy, and complete-         volume. This information is collected each year on a supplement
          ness. The States use the information to prepare State and area       to the monthly 790 or 1219 report. For an establishment
          series and then send the establishment data to the BLS for use in    making more than one product or engaging in more than one
          preparing the national series.                                       activity, the entire employment of the establishment is included

                                                                               activity.
          Shuttle schedules                                                        All national, State, and area employment, hours, earnings,
                                                                               job vacancy, and labor turnover series are classified in accord-
              Two types of data collection schedules are used: Form BLS        ance with the Standard Industrial Classification Manual, Bureau

          Turnover. These schedules are of the "shuttle" type, with space

            Industry hours and earnings                                               Average hourly earnings are on a "gross" basis, reflecting not
                                                                                  only changes in basic hourly and incentive wage rates but also
               Hours and earnings data are derived from reports of payrolls       such variable factors as premium pay for overtime and late-shift
           and man-hours for production and related workers in manu-              work and changes in output of workers paid on an incentive
           facturing and mining, construction workers in contract construc-       plan. Shifts in the volume of employment between relatively
           tion, and nonsupervisory employees in the remaining private            high-paid and low-paid work and changes in workers' earnings in

           reported is longer than .1 week, figures are reduced to a weekly           Averages of hourly earnings differ from wage rates. Earnings
           basis.                                                                 are the actual return to the worker for a stated period of time;

           all nonsupervisory workers (including lead men and trainees)           The earnings series does not measure the level of total labor costs
           engaged in fabricating, processing, assembling, inspection,            on the part of the employer since the following are excluded:
           receiving, storage, handling, packing, warehousing, shipping,          Irregular bonuses, retroactive items, payments of various welfare
           maintenance, repair, janitorial and watchman services, product         benefits, payroll taxes paid by employers, and earnings for those
           development, auxiliary production for plant's own use (e.g.,           employees not covered under the production-worker,
           power plant), and recordkeeping and other services closely             construction-worker, or nonsupervisory-employee definitions.
           associated with the above production operations.                           Gross average weekly earnings are derived by multiplying
               Construction workers include the following employees in            average weekly hours by average hourly earnings. Therefore,

           working supervisory level) such as office and clerical workers,            Long-term trends of gross average weekly earnings can be

                           Additional information concerning the preparation of the labor force, employment, hours, earnings,
                           job vacancy, and labor turnover series-concepts and scope, survey methods, and limitations—is con-
                           tained in the Handbook of Methods, BLS Bulletin 1711.
